subject: Chemistry
title: Neon: The Light of the City
description: A quick, practical guide to Neon: The Light of the City.

hook.text: We call all glowing signs 'neon lights,' but real neon glows only one color: reddish-orange.

buildup.text: William Ramsay and Morris Travers discovered neon in 1898 by chilling air and separating its gases.

discovery.text: When they ran electricity through it, neon glowed a brilliant crimson red. Travers called it 'a blaze of crimson.'

twist.text: Georges Claude made the first neon sign in 1910. By the 1930s, neon lit up Times Square and Las Vegas.

climax.text: Other gas colors—blue for mercury, green for argon—get called 'neon' too, but only neon makes red.

punchline.text: Only reddish-orange is real neon. Everything else is an imposter.

quiz.question: What color does pure neon gas produce when electrified?
